svg.title: How to read an EXPLAIN PLAN in 30 seconds: indentation is parentage, the deepest leaves run first, and three landmark shapes tell you the story
svg.text: HOW TO READ A PLAN IN 30 SECONDS
SELECT STATEMENT
SORT ORDER BY STOPKEY
the finisher: STOPKEY = only the top-N rows
survive — this is your ORDER BY … FETCH FIRST
HASH JOIN
the combiners: joins merge two child
row sources — one from each indent below
TABLE ACCESS FULL MENU_ITEMS
the leaves: tables and indexes actually read.
Deepest leaves execute FIRST, then feed upward
INDEX RANGE SCAN ORDERS_IX
Rule 1 — indentation is parentage. Each operation feeds the one it is indented under.
Rule 2 — children run first. Execution starts at the deepest leaves and flows up to the SELECT.
Rule 3 — hunt landmarks, not rows. Leaves say WHAT was read; joins say HOW it was combined;
STOPKEY says only the best N survived. Costs and row counts vary — the SHAPE is the story.
Why this matters in a converged database: every data model appears in this ONE tree as ordinary
row sources — graph MATCHes, JSON documents, vector distances — all planned by the same optimizer.
In a portfolio of engines there is no such tree, because no planner can see across the seams.
Next: find the three numbered landmarks in the capstone's real plan below.
